title: A Guide To Stablecoins: Multi-Collateral-Backed Stablecoins—DAI, USDS

A Guide To Stablecoins: Multi-Collateral-Backed Stablecoins—DAI, USDS

A Guide To Stablecoins: Multi-Collateral-Backed Stablecoins—DAI, USDS

June 25, 2026
41 min read

By: Raye Hadi

Research Associate, Digital Assets

More from Raye Hadi

Join the conversation

Raye Hadi joined ARK in April 2025 as a Research Associate, where he focuses on identifying and analyzing cryptoassets that are disrupting legacy financial systems– ranging from L1 and L2 networks to DeFi protocols, RWAs, DePIN, etc.

Prior to ARK, Raye worked as a Strategy Analyst at Horizen Labs Ventures, a token advisory firm specializing in tokenomics design. He also held research roles at Sazmining, a Bitcoin mining-as-a-service provider, and The Mechanism Institute, a research group advancing onchain mechanism design. Raye began his crypto journey by co-founding his university’s blockchain club, where he served as President and Research Lead for over a year.

Raye graduated with honors from the University of Virginia, earning a Bachelor’s degree in Economics with a minor in Computer Science.

IntroductionIn Part One of ARK’s four-part guide to stablecoins, we introduced stablecoins and presented the design concept and history that contextualize their development. I argued that each type of stablecoin design presents tradeoffs and consequences for holders.This article focuses on the stablecoin category called “Multi-Collateral-Backed Stablecoins” and delves into how reserves are managed, how yield and incentives are distributed, how accessible and integrated the tokens are, and how governance and compliance influence their resilience.The Multi-Collateral-Backed Stablecoins covered here are:DAI (Sky Protocol)USDS (Sky Protocol)Considered the original crypto-native stablecoin type, multi-collateral-backed stablecoins peaked in late 2021 at ~7% of the stablecoin market and have dropped to ~4% market share.1 Despite early adoption throughout Decentralized Finance (DeFi) markets, multi-collateral-backed stablecoins never hit escape velocity because of capital inefficiencies stemming from an initial reliance on overcollateralized vaults. In response to that limitation, the multi-collateral framework has evolved to support not only more efficient methods of collateralization, but also more collateral types. The largest issuer of this type, Sky Protocol, has been the dominant force behind this evolution, transitioning half its stablecoin supply to support these new strategic objectives. Because they are not compliant with the GENIUS Act’s definition of payments stablecoins, multi-collateral stablecoins are at a relative disadvantage to their majority fiat-backed counterparts. That said, by shifting toward reserve diversification, Real World Assets (RWAs), and tokenization, multi-collateral-backed stablecoins are positioning to compete for new utility amid evolving stablecoin demand.In this analysis, we present our assessment of the Multi-Collateral-Backed stablecoins DAI and USDS based on our five key criteria:Transparency: Clarity and verifiability of reserves, real-time tracking, and user-led analysisDurability: Peg stability, insurance mechanisms, external dependencies, and ability to preserve valueSovereignty: Censorship and seizure resistance, trust surface, and real ownershipAccessibility: Usability, integrations, liquidity, and user experienceIncentives: Native mechanisms that either reward holders or pass value back to usersMulti-Collateral-Backed StablecoinsMulti-collateral-backed stablecoins are more complex and dynamic compared to majority-fiat backed stablecoins. Their value derives not from centralized, fiat-denominated assets alone but from multiple onchain collateral types, including crypto assets, fiat-backed stablecoins, and real-world assets (RWAs). The primary examples in this category are DAI and USDS, both from the Sky Protocol (formerly MakerDAO protocol). Although their mechanisms are intertwined by the Multi-Collateral DAI (MCD) framework and shared architecture, their risk profiles and censorship resistance diverge because of differences in token design and governance.The MCD FrameworkThe Multi-Collateral DAI (MCD) framework originally was built on a collateralized debt position (CDP) model, in which users deposited ERC-20 assets2 into Ethereum smart contracts to mint the DAI stablecoin as debt. To mitigate risk, this model required a collateral-to-debt ratio of >1, meaning that users had to borrow less than they deposited. In other words, it was not capital efficient. Initially, the framework supported only ETH as collateral in a system known as the Single-Collateral DAI (SCD).3 As it matured, the protocol supported additional assets, marking the transition from SCD to MCD.Based on a strategic pivot to expand the protocol’s offerings and move away from the capital inefficient MCD framework toward the growing real-world asset (RWA) and tokenization markets, MakerDAO evolved into the Sky Protocol and launched USDS, a stablecoin built on the same underlying architecture and MCD framework as DAI but designed specifically to integrate RWA-backed collateral and orchestrate more efficient collateralization methods.The Vat ContractThe Vat.dai smart contract is a shared architecture that powers Sky Protocol’s (formerly MakerDAO) stablecoin standard.4 With an internal ledger and foundational logic, the contract exists on Ethereum and powers the protocol. By design, the Vat does not interface with any external applications and instead uses ERC-20 token contracts as wrappers for interacting with the protocol. DAI and USDS are examples of a wrapper around a Vat.dai balance, entitling holders to a $1 worth of reserves hooked into the Vat. All of Sky Protocol’s stablecoin design is built on top of the Vat contract, making it critical to the overall design of a multi-collateral-backed stablecoin system and to the mechanisms upon which DAI and USDS operate.  TransparencyComposition Of ReservesThe composition of Sky Protocol’s reserves backing both USDS and DAI reflects a variety of assets. By design, the composition is broad but structured, ranging from a variety of onchain assets to tokenized real world assets (RWAs). The two dominant components are the Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL) and Stablecoins, representing ~28% and ~30% of total reserves, respectively.5 The remaining 42% is allocated across protocol-wide mechanisms, including Core Collateralized Debt Position (CDP) Vaults, Grove, the Staking Engine, Legacy RWAs, and other newer initiatives, such as Obex. These reserves predominantly are embedded in Ethereum smart contracts, with a small percentage on other Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) networks, as shown below. Several of these components are run by capital allocators known as "Agents" (formerly "Stars"), which are semi-independent entities that borrow USDS from Sky and deploy it into governance-approved yield strategies, with Spark, Grove, and Obex the most active.Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026, based on data from SkyEcosystem as of June 11, 2026.6 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.While Core CDP Vaults were the original backing for multi-collateral stablecoin reserves, as MakerDAO has transitioned to Sky Protocol the reserves have changed notably. With support for RWAs, and thus USDS, reserves now range from stablecoins, to protocol-owned liquidity (PoL), to tokenized assets.The vat.dai contract manages the collateral types and can be upgraded through governance initiatives that allow for new collateral types, such as PoL and RWAs, to be onboarded without affecting the DAI or USDS token contracts. DAI and USDS share one unified backing, with no distinction between which assets back DAI and which back USDS in Sky Protocol. While the collateral backing each token is effectively the same, individual reserve types serve distinct roles, some of which only one token can support. The table below illustrates the different reserve components and their function:DescriptionStablecoinsDeployment of stablecoins (mostly USDC) into the Sky LitePSM (Peg Stability Module) smart contract. This enables users to act as liquidity providers, facilitating seamless swaps between DAI/USDC and USDS/USDC.Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL)A capital deployment layer within Spark Protocol (governed by Sky Protocol) that enables Sky to actively deploy stablecoins into onchain yield-generating protocols (primarily SparkLend, Morpho, Curve, and Maple) to minimize idle capital and generate organic DeFi yield. Protocols subject to change*Core CDP VaultsNative Ethereum smart contracts that allow users to deposit Sky-approved crypto assets and mint DAI or USDS against them through overcollateralized debt positions.GroveA Sky Agent focused on institutional credit governed by Sky Protocol that enables USDS issuance backed by tokenized private credit and offchain lending instruments.Staking EngineA vault system for users to lock up SKY tokens (governance token of Sky protocol) to borrow against USDS, earn rewards, and engage in Sky governance.Legacy RWATokenized US Treasuries and other legacy real-world assets (RWAs) used as onchain collateral to mint USDS.OthersConsists of newer Agents in the Sky ecosystem such as Obex, a stablecoin incubator designed to finance yield generating stablecoins built on Sky protocol, and Keel, a a capital allocator initially focused on Solana, since broadened to EVM and TradFi.Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026, based on data from several sources as indicated in endnote.7 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.The Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL) represents the second largest portion of reserves and comprises five main stablecoins: USDT, PYUSD, USDS, USDC, and DAI. These reserves are known as “Protocol-Owned Liquidity” (PoL), as governance manages the balance. This enables Sky to generate protocol level yield to support its broader ecosystem while, at the same time, maintaining the flexibility among different stablecoins and protocols seamlessly. At the time of this writing in June 2026, most SLL liquidity was deployed in yield-generating protocols such as: Sparklend, Maple, Curve, and Morpho. The current distribution of the SLL’s deployment can be tracked using the link below:SLL Distribution: https://data.spark.fi/spark-liquidity-layerLinks to stablecoin issuer websites are provided for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.In contrast, Core CDP Vaults are user-owned, overcollateralized smart contracts in which borrowers lock assets and mint either DAI or USDS. Like all collateral in the protocol, the deposited assets are connected to the vat.dai contract and add to the protocol’s backing. DAI or USDS tokens received act as credit entitling the holder to a claim the size of the deposit. While the positions are segregated at the user-level, the shared architecture of the vat.dai contract obfuscates differences at the protocol layer. As a result, discerning between the CDP collateral behind DAI versus USDS is impractical.The remaining reserve segments are critical to Sky Protocol’s resilience and offchain operations. The Stablecoin Stability module (Sky’s LitePSM) supports liquidity, conversions, and peg-stability across both USDS and DAI into USDC. The Staking Engine supports governance engagement and USDS demand. Newer Agents like Obex and Keel incubate stablecoin growth and provide additional yield opportunities. Meanwhile, Grove and Legacy RWAs bring offchain credit and tokenized US Treasuries onchain, enabling native RWA backing. While those capabilities are not exclusive to USDS, the USDS token contract has the capability to support compliant interaction with RWA-based reserves. Offchain reserves reflect the broader design intention to diversify the collateral base and integrate institutional-grade collateral.Transparency Of ReservesUnlike fiat-backed stablecoins that rely on third-party attestations, DAI and USDS operate entirely onchain, enabling real-time, verifiable transparency into their reserves. Both stablecoins are issued through fully transparent smart contracts, with the underlying collateral traceable on the Ethereum blockchain and other networks that host it. Another key architectural feature is that the same backing represents both DAI and USDS, despite the fact that RWA collateral types require permissioned controls impossible to enforce through the DAI token contract. As a result, even though 100% of their reserves are visible onchain, the reserves are fully aggregated at the smart contract level (blockchain ledger), making it impossible to attribute any specific reserve to DAI versus USDS; backing is verifiable only in aggregate.Dashboard:https://info.sky.money/collateralLinks to stablecoin issuer websites are provided for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.DurabilityDepegging Risk And External DependenciesLike all stablecoins, the peg stability of multi collateral-backed stablecoins like DAI and USDS depends on factors both endogenous and exogenous to the stablecoin design. In terms of endogenous risk, the peg stability of DAI and USDS depends on the quality of their collateral, the operational integrity of Sky Protocol, and the reliability of supporting infrastructure like oracles and bridges. Because both stablecoins interface with the same internal balance system (vat.dai), all systemic risks within the protocol—such as collateral seizure, governance mismanagement, or reckless DeFi exposure—impact each stablecoin equally. Major depegging risks include:Widespread collateral failure (ETH crash, RWA default, USDC freeze)Oracle manipulation or outages leading to unfair liquidationsGovernance failure like malicious votes or coordination breakdownSmart contract bugs or hacksExogenous risks are those which occur outside the protocol’s control, typically in external venues. Those risks can cause the token price to depeg locally, even if the protocol is fully operational and internal collateral is fully solvent. An example of exchange-specific conditions causing a local depeg can be found in Part 2 of ARK’s Stablecoin Guide: Majority Fiat-Backed Stablecoins: USDT, USDC, PYUSD. Other major depegging risks include:Bridge failuresIsolated venue/exchange outagesExtreme market panicImportantly, these are venue-specific or external price deviations, not protocol solvency problems. The protocol could continue to function perfectly, even if users experience a temporary local depegging.In cross-chain contexts, Sky does not track DAI or USDS held outside Ethereum. If those assets are lost because of a bridge failure or are compromised on another network, the impact is isolated to that chain. Reserves on Ethereum remain unaffected, and any governance intervention is optional, not guaranteed.If the protocol experiences insolvency, losses are absorbed through a tiered waterfall. The agent level over-collateralization sitting within each capital allocator is the first line of defense. At the time of writing, system-wide agent collateral sits at ~$13.9 billion backing ~$11.9 billion of debt (issued stablecoins), leaving agents ~116% overcollateralized.8 Should this overcollateralization margin be insufficient, protocol-level losses then draw on the Aggregate Backstop Capital (ABC), which is the Sky Surplus Buffer plus Genesis Capital held in Agent treasuries. Genesis capital refers to funds already paid out to agents that now sit in each agent’s SubProxy (treasury). Split across all agents, that comes to ~$100.84m today.9 The SubProxies are controlled by Sky Governance and are mandated to transfer those assets back to the protocol when needed for a backstop.10 However, the net loss-absorbing capacity of the ABC is not the full Genesis figure. This capacity must account for the protocol’s operational deficit/surplus known as the Surplus Buffer, referring to the protocol’s assets less liabilities. The Surplus Buffer sits at around ~-$34 million today, leaving ~$66m as the true backstop available for recovery within the ABC.11 Only when losses exceed all of ABC does the protocol turn to minting, which is known as a Sky Backstop Event. During that window, the protocol will automatically generate and sell SKY tokens on the open market to recapitalize the system. While there is a default backstop limit, in a true emergency, the limit can be overridden and the mint is uncapped. If the backstop limit is hit and not overridden, or the backstop is halted, or losses still exceed what SKY minting can absorb, the USDS target price is adjusted below $1 to settle the remaining bad debt.12Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.Between a healthy overcollateralization ratio and sufficient ABC balance, any trigger of a backstop event becomes highly unlikely, giving Sky a robust insolvency framework. The protocol’s available Backstop Capital and Aggregate Agent Overcollateralization Ratio can be tracked in the following dashboards:Backstop Capital (ABC): https://info.sky.money/capital-backstopAgent Overcollateralization Ratio: https://info.skyeco.com/collateralLinks to stablecoin issuer websites are provided for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.Peg Stability: Mint/Redemption MechanismsWhen MakerDAO initially launched DAI under its SCD framework, user-driven mint/redeem arbitrage via core CDP vaults was the primary mechanism for peg stability.13 Upon its transition to a MCD framework and the onboarding of USDC reserves, the protocol shifted away from that method of peg stabilization. As of the latest reserve snapshot, only ~12% of backing for DAI and USDS now comes from core CDP vaults,14 the majority of the balance is backed by modules like the Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL), Staking Engine, Grove, and stablecoins in the Peg Stability Module (LitePSM), all of which rely more on protocol-controlled liquidity than on market-driven arbitrage.Today, peg stability is managed primarily through the Peg Stability Module (PSM), now known as The LitePSM. The LitePSM refers to the ~30% of reserves in stablecoins, and it allows users to swap between DAI or USDS and USDC at a fixed rate, minus small governance-set fees. This mechanism ties DAI and USDS directly to USDC liquidity, giving both stablecoins a mechanism for restabilizing within the market:If DAI or USDS trades above $1, users can deposit USDC into the LitePSM and receive DAI/USDS plus a fee, increasing supply and pressuring the price back down.If trading below $1, users can redeem DAI or USDS for USDC via the LitePSM, reducing supply and helping restore the peg.To simplify liquidity across both tokens, the LitePSM has two modules: LitePSM-DAI-USDC and LitePSMWrapper-USDS-USDC. The DAI-USDC module holds DAI and actual USDC liquidity, while the USDS-USDC wrapper does not hold any USDC liquidity and routes all USDS conversions internally through the DAI-USDC module.15 That setup allows both DAI and USDS to share the same pool of USDC and avoid liquidity fragmentation, making the system more efficient. While the LitePSM is low-friction and accessible without know your customer (KYC), protocol governance can pause or cap redemptions/swaps. Currently, ~4.1 billion USDC are in the LitePSM, which can be tracked here:https://litepsm.blockanalitica.com/Links to stablecoin issuer websites are provided for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.Coinbase Prime reportedly is the custody and Multi-Party Computation (MPC) provider behind the account that holds and can move the USDC reserves backing the LitePSM, though it neither operates nor governs the module itself, as those functions remain with Sky's smart contracts and governance.16While minting/redeeming DAI or USDS through core CDP vaults remains active and open to users via interfaces like DeFi Saver and Summer.fi, it plays little role in peg stabilization. Instead, CDP vaults serve as debt issuance vehicles, protocol liquidity management—primarily through LitePSM and Sky-controlled USDS minting via Allocator Vaults—handles peg stabilization. Peg maintenance now depends on:The presence of sufficient USDC in LitePSM contractsUSDC independently maintaining its own pegProtocol governance managing buffers, fees, and liquidity provisioningSky’s ability to adjust system parameters and maintain confidence in its infrastructureSovereigntyGovernanceSky Protocol governance operates under a framework inherited by MakerDAO called the “Atlas-Based Model.”17 The system enables SKY token holders either to vote directly on governance proposals or to delegate their voting power to Aligned Delegates (ADs)—entities that represent specific governance strategies and vote in accordance with their stated vision.If an AD deviates from its mandate, token holders can redelegate at any time, stripping the AD of voting power and associated rewards. This dynamic makes delegate concentration more impactful than token holder distribution. The current distribution of voting power among active delegates is shown below:Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026, based on data from Vote Sky as of June 11, 2026.18 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.Currently, two ADs—cloaky and Blue—account for more than 75% of delegated SKY,19 giving them supermajority control over governance decisions: they can pass or block almost any vote. While that dominance has caused centralization concerns, ADs can maintain dominance only by continuing to vote in the best interests of their delegators. The breakdown of Sky delegate distribution can be tracked here:https://vote.sky.money/delegatesLinks to stablecoin issuer websites are provided for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.Sky governance controls protocol-level decisions, including protocol fees, collateral types, and risk parameters. That said, DAI and USDS have different degrees of control because of their underlying contract designs, leading to some governance features that apply only to USDS, as described in the table below:Governance FeatureToken AffectedDescription And Upgradability ImpactTime Delay (Pause Proxy)DAI & USDSMost governance actions are subject to a governance-set delay, currently 24 hours, before execution. This gives the community time to respond to potential changes. Applies to all actions including direct contract upgrades. Some emergency actions can be routed through IAM and avoid this execution delay.Instant Access Modules (IAMs)/MOMsDAI & USDSPre-approved parameters (IAMs) can adjust automatically within preset bounds without a vote or delay, while emergency circuit breakers (Mom contracts) let governance bypass the delay for protective actions. These modules cannot alter token contracts directly.Collateral Whitelisting And Parameter ChangesDAI & USDSGovernance can onboard new collateral types and adjust risk parameters (LTVs, debt ceilings, stability fees*). These changes are critical to mitigating protocol risk exposure. Changes happen at the Vat contract layer and therefore do not alter token contracts.Governance Induced Modification Of The Token ContractUSDSSky governance has the authority to upgrade or modify the USDS token contract and add new functionality (transfers, balances, mint/burn logic, freeze/blacklist permissions).Note: Stability fees are the interest rates borrowers pay on their DAI or USDS positions, determined by the specific collateral type deposited. Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026.20 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.Causing some differences between the two stablecoins, the USDS ERC20 token contract was designed to be upgradeable with community approval, while DAI was designed to be immutable, making it immune to alterations, upgrades, or forceful deprecation, as confirmed by founder Rune Christensen. An attempt to “upgrade” DAI would create an entirely new token that the community would have to adopt voluntarily.Ultimately, the DAI ERC20 contract and USDS ERC20 token contract are wrappers for the internal vat.dai balance. As a result, most governance decisions do not alter DAI or USDS token contracts; instead, they make alterations to the vat like adding new collateral types. Sky governance has the authority to modify or upgrade USDS but cannot change existing DAI. DAI’s immutability makes structural updates contingent upon ecosystem-wide adoption of new token contracts. USDS does not face that limitation. Confirmation of DAI’s immutability by founder Rune Christensen can be accessed here:https://x.com/RuneKek/status/1828460336787177557Links to stablecoin issuer websites are provided for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.ComplianceDAI is built as a decentralized, immutable smart contract. As a result, Sky Protocol has no direct ability to enforce compliance on DAI users. Alternatively, USDS was designed to support real-world asset (RWA) collateral, which requires regulatory features like KYC/ anti-money laundering (AML), blacklisting, and address freezing. While not active currently, the USDS contract includes upgradability hooks that support freezing, subject to a governance vote.21 Because of their shared underlying Vat architecture, both tokens act as interfaces giving users the option to choose the way they interact with the protocol: USDS for those prioritizing compliance, and DAI for those preferring a more unconstrained experience.The differences between DAI and USDS give Sky Protocol the tools to manage compliance risks related to borrowing against RWAs while preserving user optionality. As a result, USDS is more controllable and less censorship-resistant than DAI. Conversely, DAI’s immutability makes it more resistant to centralized intervention. Sky Protocol cannot freeze collateral, blacklist addresses, or seize DAI funds; nor can it upgrade the native token contract. That said, those features do not immunize DAI from indirect censorship risk. Most of the collateral backing DAI resides in assets subject to freezes/censorship, such as fiat-backed stablecoins, RWAs, and PoL.Accordingly, a portion of DAI’s backing could become inaccessible, impairing peg stability. If frozen, the DAI token would remain usable, with less collateral backing it potentially impairing peg stability. Additionally, Sky Protocol governance could remove or reclassify the collateral types it manages, influencing DAI’s peg indirectly.While they are edge cases and would apply systemically, those risks highlight that DAI provides stronger censorship resistance with minimal compliance, but remains dependent on its collateral exposure and on the parties that control that collateral.AccessibilityLiquidityDAI and USDS have crossed $1 billion in supply on only one blockchain,22 Ethereum, and despite having launched less than two years ago, USDS is now more liquid onchain than DAI, as shown in the charts below. A recent wave of centralized exchange migrations occurring through early- to mid- 2026 has reshaped the liquidity picture.23 Many large exchanges executed automatic 1:1 conversions of user DAI balances into USDS and delisted DAI. As a result, DAI's onchain presence is now concentrated in DeFi while USDS has become the dominant instrument of Sky across centralized venues and even decentralized venues as well. That said, both are significantly smaller than Majority Fiat-Backed leaders, USDT and USDC.Disclaimer: Values reflect onchain stablecoin balances custodied by wallets associated with each venue, aggregated from exchange reserve disclosures, DEX explorers, and third-party wallet clustering providers. These figures do not represent tradable liquidity, order-book depth, or execution capacity, particularly for centralized exchanges where most trading occurs offchain via internal ledgers. On-chain balances may over-or under-state a venue's effective liquidity depending on off-chain custody practices and wallet attribution methodologies. As of June 2026. Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026, based on data from multiple sources as indicated in endnote25 as of June 11, 2026. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.Source: ARK Investment Management, 2026, based on data from ArtemisAI.24 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.IntegrationsDAI and USDS are available across all major EVM chains and supported on most exchanges and DeFi protocols, along with native USDS on Solana—an integration announced in 2024 through Wormhole’s Native Token Transfer (NTT) infrastructure.26 Native minting and redemption, however, can occur only on Ethereum mainnet, where all vault contracts and protocol logic reside. Users must open and close CDP positions on Ethereum using canonical DAI or USDS. Moreover, collateral exists solely on Ethereum in the Vat contract.While the Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL) extends the reserves of DAI and USDS across Base, Unichain, Arbitrum, and others,27 where they are usable, the core protocol is functional only on Ethereum. IncentivesYield And IncentivesWhile DAI and USDS in their base form technically are not “yield-bearing stablecoins,” Sky Protocol generates revenue from the protocol’s collateral (RWAs, SLL), as well as stability fees paid by borrowers who collateralize their assets to mint either DAI or USDS. A growing share of that revenue comes from Sky lending USDS to capital allocators called "Agents" (formerly "Stars") at a set base rate; the Agents then deploy that capital into yield strategies and are responsible for earning a spread above what they paid to borrow it, allowing Sky to generate revenue at the lending layer while pushing allocation decisions outward.28 One such Agent is the Spark Lending Protocol, which acts as a quasi-native yield layer. Spark is governed by the Sky DAO and is tied directly to Sky’s reserve and liquidity systems that provide direct access to yield mechanisms endogenous to the Sky ecosystem. Spark’s yield-bearing modules consist mainly of:Lending markets for a variety of onchain assetsThe Sky Savings Rate module:DAI → sDAI, Dai Savings Rate (DSR)USDS → sUSDS, Sky Savings Rate (SSR)sDAI and sUSDS are yield-bearing tokens that allow users to deposit DAI or USDS and earn a portion of the revenue accrued by the Sky protocol, without losing the ability to deploy their assets across DeFi.29 For users seeking alternative returns, DAI and USDS also can be deployed into external yield-bearing DeFi markets like Spark, Morpho, Aave, and Maple. A good resource for understanding the current yield opportunities available to DAI and USDS across different ecosystems can be accessed here:https://stablecoins.asxn.xyz/crypto-yieldsLinks to stablecoin issuer websites are provided for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security or cryptocurrency.ConclusionMulti-collateral-backed stablecoins introduced complexity compared to majority fiat-backed stablecoin designs, but they are critical to more decentralized and composable forms of digital money. By leveraging many different types of onchain assets, these ecosystems offer much higher transparency and crypto-native properties than other models. The native onchain architecture of both the protocol mechanisms and reserves enables not only actively managed revenue generation from the collateral, but also highly accessible and low-friction USDC redemptions for any DAI or USDS holder. Sky Protocol makes both possible through the Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL) and LitePSM modules, providing not only real time verification but also transparency across DeFi.With the launch of USDS, Sky created a compliant way to interface with the protocol on an opt-in basis. Sky recognized the evolving stablecoin landscape and has positioned the protocol’s roadmap accordingly. Multi-Collateral-Backed stablecoins do not meet the criteria to be GENIUS Act-compliant “payments stablecoins,” but they still should enjoy some of the benefits of the legislation. Their native onchain presence and their ability to leverage the stability of majority-fiat backed stablecoins are enhancing the flexibility and efficiency of crypto-native operations, especially given the durable reserve backing that includes Majority Fiat-Backed stablecoins. As the landscape matures, multi-collateral-backed stablecoins should continue to evolve and optimize their design and reserves for both market-fit and revenue generation.Since the dawn of DeFi, Multi-Collateral-Backed stablecoins have been integral to the development of onchain finance. As this space continues to merge with traditional finance, crypto-native options are positioning to grow the financial markets and compete. Most notably, USDS demonstrates how this design can pass protocol revenue back to holders through the Sky Savings Rate, offering a differentiated, natively yield-bearing instrument that GENIUS Act payments stablecoins cannot replicate.Part IV of ARK’s Stablecoin Guide will turn to synthetic dollar stablecoins—a novel design that ensures price stability through market dynamics, algorithmic feedback, and endogenous economic incentives. This design aims to achieve the same composable, crypto-native stablecoin without an overcollateralized, CDP design.Important InformationStablecoins aim to maintain a stable value, but they are not risk-free. Their value and stability depend on the issuer’s reserves, governance, and market conditions. Factors such as reserve mismanagement, regulatory changes, technical vulnerabilities, or loss of market confidence may cause a stablecoin to lose its peg or become illiquid. Users should conduct their own research, understand the underlying mechanisms and risks, and not rely on stablecoins as a guaranteed store of value.Glossary Of Terms“DeFi” or decentralized finance is an emerging financial system using blockchain and cryptocurrencies to enable direct transactions between individuals and businesses. “CeFi” or centralized finance refers to financial services and platforms provided by centralized entities, such as banks and exchanges, that manage user accounts and transactions.Fiat currency is a type of government-issued currency, authorized by government regulation to be legal tender. The US dollar is an example of fiat currency.The “Genius Act” is a US federal law (passed in 2025) that creates a national regulatory framework for “payment stablecoins”—digital tokens meant to hold a stable value (typically $1) and be used for payments/settlement.

See also Spark. 2026. “Spark Liquidity Layer.” Sky. 2026. “LitePSM.” “Key Functionalities (as defined in the smart contract).” Sky. 2026. “Staking Engine (LockStake Engine).” Wintermeyer, L. 2025. “Obex Set To Be The Y-Combinator For Stablecoin Startups With $2.5 Billion Mandate.” Forbes.
